is a [sprint](/wiki/Sprint_(running) "Sprint (running)") covering 40 yards (36.576 m). It is primarily run to evaluate the [speed](/wiki/Speed "Speed") and acceleration of [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") players by [scouts](/wiki/Scout_(sport) "Scout (sport)"), particularly for the [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") but also for collegiate [recruiting](/wiki/Recruiting_(athletics) "Recruiting (athletics)"). A football player's recorded time can have a heavy impact on his prospects in college or professional football. This was traditionally only true for the skill positions such as [running back](/wiki/Running_back "Running back"), [wide receiver](/wiki/Wide_receiver "Wide receiver"), and [defensive back](/wiki/Defensive_back "Defensive back"), although now a fast 40-yard dash time is considered important for almost every position. The 40-yard dash is not an official race in [track and field athletics](/wiki/Track_and_field_athletics "Track and field athletics"), and is not a [World Athletics](/wiki/World_Athletics "World Athletics")–recognized race.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Speed test in American football

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Buckley_Participates_in_USAA_Salute_to_Service_Boot_Camp_%288607868%29.jpg/330px-Buckley_Participates_in_USAA_Salute_to_Service_Boot_Camp_%288607868%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buckley_Participates_in_USAA_Salute_to_Service_Boot_Camp_(8607868).jpg)

A [United States airman](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") running a 40-yard dash

The **40-yard dash** is a [sprint](/wiki/Sprint_(running) "Sprint (running)") covering 40 yards (36.576 m). It is primarily run to evaluate the [speed](/wiki/Speed "Speed") and acceleration of [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") players by [scouts](/wiki/Scout_(sport) "Scout (sport)"), particularly for the [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") but also for collegiate [recruiting](/wiki/Recruiting_(athletics) "Recruiting (athletics)"). A football player's recorded time can have a heavy impact on his prospects in college or professional football. This was traditionally only true for the skill positions such as [running back](/wiki/Running_back "Running back"), [wide receiver](/wiki/Wide_receiver "Wide receiver"), and [defensive back](/wiki/Defensive_back "Defensive back"), although now a fast 40-yard dash time is considered important for almost every position. The 40-yard dash is not an official race in [track and field athletics](/wiki/Track_and_field_athletics "Track and field athletics"), and is not a [World Athletics](/wiki/World_Athletics "World Athletics")–recognized race.

The origin of timing football players for 40 yards comes from the average distance of a [punt](/wiki/Punt_(gridiron_football) "Punt (gridiron football)") and the time it takes to reach that distance.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Punts average around 40 yards in distance from the [line of scrimmage](/wiki/Line_of_scrimmage "Line of scrimmage"), and the hangtime (time of flight) averages approximately 4.5 seconds; therefore, if a player can run 40 yards in 4.5 seconds, he will be able to leave the line of scrimmage when a punt is kicked, and reach the point where the ball comes down just as it arrives.

## Timing method and track comparisons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40-yard_dash&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Timing method and track comparisons")]

In terms of judging a person's speed, the best method of timing is through lasers which start and stop the times when passed through. A laser start (from a stationary position) is more accurate for measuring pure speed as it does not register a runner's reaction time, however, this method of timing a 40-yard dash can affect the accuracy by as much as 0.5 seconds with the manual [stopwatch](/wiki/Stopwatch "Stopwatch") method.

The [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) did not begin using partial electronic timing (i.e. started by hand, stopped electronically) at the [NFL Scouting Combine](/wiki/NFL_Scouting_Combine "NFL Scouting Combine") until 1999.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) For purposes of measurement at the Combine, the run is made along the sideline from the front of the [end zone](/wiki/End_zone "End zone") to the 40-yard line, and for electronically timed 40-yard dashes, the runner is allowed to start when they wish, and a timer hand-starts the clock.

In contrast, [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") races have the runner [react](/wiki/Reaction_time "Reaction time") to a [starting gun](/wiki/Starting_pistol "Starting pistol"). Elite male sprinters typically take 0.15 to 0.2 seconds (based on [FAT timing](/wiki/FAT_timing "FAT timing")) to react; further to this, IAAF rules state any runner with a reaction time of less than 0.1 second is subject to disqualification.

This aspect means that comparisons with track times are essentially impossible given that a reaction time is not factored in, and the use of hand-timing in the 40-yard dash can considerably alter a runner's time: the methods are not comparable to the rigorous electronic timing used in track and field.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)

For example, [Jacoby Ford](/wiki/Jacoby_Ford "Jacoby Ford"), who ran 4.28 seconds in the 2010 NFL Combine, had a collegiate best of 6.51 s in the [60-meter dash](/wiki/60-meter_dash "60-meter dash") (outside the top-40 of the all-time lists).[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)

Though not a current event, the 40-yard dash was briefly contested at the [USA Indoor Track and Field Championships](/wiki/USA_Indoor_Track_and_Field_Championships "USA Indoor Track and Field Championships") for women in [1927](/wiki/1927_USA_Indoor_Track_and_Field_Championships "1927 USA Indoor Track and Field Championships"), [1928](/wiki/1928_USA_Indoor_Track_and_Field_Championships "1928 USA Indoor Track and Field Championships"), [1929](/wiki/1929_USA_Indoor_Track_and_Field_Championships "1929 USA Indoor Track and Field Championships"), [1930](/wiki/1930_USA_Indoor_Track_and_Field_Championships "1930 USA Indoor Track and Field Championships"), [1931](/wiki/1931_USA_Indoor_Track_and_Field_Championships "1931 USA Indoor Track and Field Championships"), and [1932](/wiki/1932_USA_Indoor_Track_and_Field_Championships "1932 USA Indoor Track and Field Championships"). It was never staged as a men's event. The fastest winning time, including reaction, was 5.2 (originally recorded as 51⁄5) seconds, first set by [Rosa Grosse](/w/index.php?title=Rosa_Grosse&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rosa Grosse (page does not exist)") and later tied by [Mary Carew](/wiki/Mary_Carew "Mary Carew") twice.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40-yard_dash&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Records")]

In most settings, the 40-yard dash is conducted without [fully automatic timing](/wiki/Fully_automatic_time "Fully automatic time"), where lasers are used at both the beginning and end of the race.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Instead, the 40-yard dash is most often hand-timed, leading to considerable measurement error. Many (in particular older) reports of times below 4.2 or 4.3 are considered suspect, such as Baylor's [Gerald McNeil](/wiki/Gerald_McNeil "Gerald McNeil")'s 4.19-second 40-yard dash in the 1980s before being signed to the [United States Football League](/wiki/United_States_Football_League "United States Football League") (USFL),[[7]](#cite_note-7) or [Deion Sanders](/wiki/Deion_Sanders "Deion Sanders")'s 4.27-second 40-yard dash in 1989.[[8]](#cite_note-8) More recent examples include rugby union's [Carlin Isles](/wiki/Carlin_Isles "Carlin Isles") time of 4.22 at a Detroit Lions facility during a 2013 workout,[[9]](#cite_note-9) and Texas Tech's [Jakeem Grant](/wiki/Jakeem_Grant "Jakeem Grant") being hand-timed by a [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") scout at 4.1 in 2016.[[10]](#cite_note-40yd-10)

Also unofficially, [Bo Jackson](/wiki/Bo_Jackson "Bo Jackson"), who was invited to the 1986 combine and declined, ran the 40 to show off for scouts at [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn_University "Auburn University"). Electronically, with a laser, he has said it measured 4.12, and by hand it was 4.16. "I got down there, and I took off and ran completely through. I just kept going right out the door and didn’t come back." he was quoted after.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 2017, [Olympic](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_4_%C3%97_100_metres_relay "Athletics at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Men's 4 × 100 metres relay") sprinter [Christian Coleman](/wiki/Christian_Coleman "Christian Coleman") ran a time of 4.12 seconds on turf in response to claims that NFL players are as fast as [Usain Bolt](/wiki/Usain_Bolt "Usain Bolt").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2024, [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa") sprinter Kalen Walker ran a 4.15 on turf during the halftime of a [Hawkeyes football](/wiki/Iowa_Hawkeyes_football "Iowa Hawkeyes football") game.[[13]](#cite_note-13) A year and a half after he retired from active competition, Usain Bolt ran a 4.22 in flat-soled shoes and a [tracksuit](/wiki/Tracksuit "Tracksuit") at a promotional event for the Super Bowl in Atlanta, Georgia on February 2, 2019.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### NFL Scouting Combine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40-yard_dash&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: NFL Scouting Combine")]

## Average time by position

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40-yard_dash&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Average time by position")]

According to a five-year NFL combine report, [wide receivers](/wiki/Wide_receivers "Wide receivers") and [cornerbacks](/wiki/Cornerbacks "Cornerbacks") had the fastest average times at 4.48, followed by [running backs](/wiki/Running_backs "Running backs") at 4.49. The following average times were measured between 2000 and 2012 at the NFL combine for players who played at least 5 games.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

| Position | Time |
| --- | --- |
| [Wide receiver](/wiki/Wide_receiver "Wide receiver") | 4.48 |
| [Cornerback](/wiki/Cornerback "Cornerback") | 4.48 |
| [Running back](/wiki/Running_back "Running back") | 4.49 |
| [Free safety](/wiki/Safety_(American_and_Canadian_football_position) "Safety (American and Canadian football position)") | 4.53 |
| [Strong safety](/wiki/Safety_(American_and_Canadian_football_position) "Safety (American and Canadian football position)") | 4.55 |
| [Outside linebacker](/wiki/Outside_linebacker "Outside linebacker") | 4.60 |
| [Tight end](/wiki/Tight_end "Tight end") | 4.70 |
| [Inside linebacker](/wiki/Inside_linebacker "Inside linebacker") | 4.76 |
| [Fullback](/wiki/Fullback_(American_football) "Fullback (American football)") | 4.80 |
| [Defensive end](/wiki/Defensive_end "Defensive end") | 4.80 |
| [Quarterback](/wiki/Quarterback "Quarterback") | 4.93 |
| [Defensive tackle](/wiki/Defensive_tackle "Defensive tackle") | 5.06 |
| [Center](/wiki/Center_(gridiron_football) "Center (gridiron football)") | 5.30 |
| [Offensive tackle](/wiki/Offensive_tackle "Offensive tackle") | 5.32 |
| [Offensive guard](/wiki/Offensive_guard "Offensive guard") | 5.37 |
